rationale: Infection audits directly target hospital-acquired infections — the core of patient safety.
Safety training for clinical staff directly improves patient safety outcomes.
JCI accreditation enforces clinical quality and safety standards across the hospital.
EHR can help track safety incidents but is primarily a technology initiative.
Scheduling and billing automation has no direct impact on patient safety.
Cybersecurity protects data but does not reduce clinical errors or infections.
Deploying an EHR is the primary action for modernising hospital digital systems.
Automating scheduling and billing directly modernises administrative digital workflows.
Upgrading cybersecurity is a core part of modernising hospital IT infrastructure.
Infection audits are clinical processes, not technology modernisation.
Staff safety training is a clinical initiative, not a digital technology project.
JCI accreditation requires some digital documentation but is primarily about quality standards.
